Bonsai Webgpu IntegrationHere’s the thing about WebGPU integration tutorials: they all solve the same problem. Replace the rendering backend. Swap GLSL for WGSL, rebuild your shader pipeline, ship faster rasterization. It’s a reasonable approach for many libraries.
For Bonsai.js, a browser-based graphics library built around L-system procedural tree generation, it’s the wrong optimization target entirely.
A tree scene — even a complex one — isn’t typically bottlenecked by rendering throughput. WebGL handles thin organic geometry reasonably well. The frame budget problem, when it exists, is almost always in the geometry generation step: the L-system string rewriting algorithm that runs on the CPU before anything touches the GPU.
And that’s exactly where WebGPU’s compute shaders shine.
L-system rewriting is embarrassingly parallel. Each symbol in the current-generation string can be independently rewritten according to production rules — which is a near-perfect fit for a GPU compute dispatch. One thread per symbol, input buffer in, output buffer out. The CPU-intensive bottleneck becomes a GPU-trivial workload.
This reframing changes the integration question entirely. Instead of asking “how do I replace Bonsai’s WebGL renderer?” you ask “how do I add a WebGPU compute pass that handles the generation step and hands geometry back to Bonsai’s existing renderer?” The second approach is faster to build, more architecturally stable, and actually addresses the performance problem.
Over on aithinkerlab.com, I broke down three integration patterns for this — the compute hybrid, the Three.js WebGPURenderer bridge, and the full renderer replacement — with a decision framework, honest tradeoffs, a WGSL compute shader skeleton, and the real blockers that most tutorials skip.
If you’re working on procedural web graphics and WebGPU is on your radar, the full piece is worth the read: https://aithinkerlab.com/bonsai-webgpu-integration/
